canonical_examples.analysis: Ptolemaic astronomy functioned as a paradigm: it supplied legitimate problems, mathematical techniques, standards of adequacy, and a world-picture in which celestial motion was organized around Earth. Copernican heliocentrism was not adopted simply because it solved every puzzle immediately; it reorganized the field's exemplars, standards, and ontology. The transition was a scientific revolution because key terms such as planet and motion changed their role, practitioners saw a different order in the same heavens, and the new paradigm opened a different program of normal science culminating in Kepler and Newton.
canonical_examples.problem: The Copernican Revolution displaced Ptolemaic geocentric astronomy.

canonical_examples.analysis: For much of the eighteenth century, phlogiston theory guided normal chemical research by defining combustion as the release of phlogiston and giving chemists puzzle-solutions to refine. Anomalies accumulated around weight gain in calcination and the role of gases. Lavoisier's oxygen chemistry did not merely add a correction; it changed the taxonomy of substances, the meaning of combustion, and the standards for chemical explanation. Kuhn treats this as a paradigm shift: a crisis in one framework was resolved by adopting another that made chemists work in a reorganized world.
canonical_examples.problem: Lavoisier's oxygen theory replaced phlogiston chemistry in explanations of combustion and calcination.

core_claims: Mature sciences are organized by paradigms: shared exemplars, instruments, standards, and metaphysical commitments that define legitimate puzzle-solving.
Normal science is not mainly a search for novelties; it is the articulation of a paradigm through puzzle-solving under accepted rules.
Anomalies do not falsify a paradigm one by one; they usually remain tolerated until they obstruct central work and contribute to crisis.
Scientific revolutions replace one paradigm with another, changing standards of evidence, legitimate problems, and the perceived structure of the world.
Competing paradigms can be incommensurable because their key terms, taxonomies, and exemplars do not map cleanly onto each other.
Paradigm choice is not governed by a single algorithm; accuracy, scope, simplicity, fruitfulness, and coherence matter, but scientists may weight them differently.
Textbook histories hide revolutionary discontinuities by presenting past science as a cumulative march toward the present paradigm.
description: Kuhnian Paradigm Theory models science as alternating between normal puzzle-solving within a paradigm and revolutionary episodes in which accumulated anomalies and crisis lead to an incommensurable paradigm shift. It emphasizes exemplars, disciplinary matrices, anomaly management, and historically situated standards of theory choice.
failure_modes: Relativist overreach: Kuhn's incommensurability is often misread as saying evidence never constrains paradigm choice, which erases his theory-choice values.
Revolution inflation: labeling any conceptual change a paradigm shift dilutes the framework's focus on field-wide changes in exemplars, standards, and problem spaces.
Anomaly counting: merely listing anomalies misses Kuhn's point that anomalies matter when they disrupt central puzzle-solving within a paradigm.
Neglecting normal science: practitioners may romanticize crisis and novelty while ignoring the disciplined, productive puzzle work that Kuhn treats as the bulk of mature science.
Retrospective smoothing: analysts can reproduce the textbook distortion Kuhn criticized by making a revolution look inevitable after the fact.

key_vocabulary.Anomaly: A phenomenon that resists assimilation to the reigning paradigm's expected puzzle-solutions. It becomes crisis-producing only when it is persistent and central enough to disturb normal science.
key_vocabulary.Crisis: A period in which confidence in a paradigm weakens because significant anomalies resist resolution and alternative approaches become thinkable.
key_vocabulary.Disciplinary matrix: Kuhn's later term for the shared symbolic generalizations, models, values, and exemplars that bind a scientific community.
key_vocabulary.Exemplar: A concrete problem-solution that trains practitioners in how to see and solve legitimate problems. Exemplars carry more practical authority than explicit rules.
key_vocabulary.Incommensurability: The partial non-translatability between paradigms whose taxonomies, standards, and exemplary problems differ. It makes paradigm choice comparative rather than mechanically decisive.
key_vocabulary.Normal science: Research conducted under a settled paradigm, aimed at articulating it, extending it, and solving its puzzles rather than overthrowing it.
key_vocabulary.Paradigm: A shared scientific achievement that supplies models, standards, instruments, and exemplary solutions for a community's work.
key_vocabulary.Scientific revolution: A non-cumulative transition in which one paradigm is displaced by another, reorganizing what counts as a problem, solution, and fact.

name: Kuhnian Paradigm Theory | normal science, anomaly, crisis, incommensurable paradigm shifts
operative_procedure: Identify the scientific community. Specify the field, practitioners, journals, training practices, instruments, and shared exemplars at issue.
Reconstruct the reigning paradigm. Name its central exemplars, accepted problems, standards of solution, background commitments, and normal-science agenda.
Classify the work being done. Distinguish ordinary puzzle-solving from novelty-seeking, ad hoc repair, methodological dispute, or revolutionary proposal.
Locate anomalies in paradigm terms. Ask which observations or failures resist expected solution and why they matter to the paradigm's own central commitments.
Assess crisis conditions. Determine whether anomalies are persistent, public, central, and accompanied by loss of confidence or proliferation of alternatives.
Compare rival paradigms as worlds of practice. Examine how each organizes terms, taxonomies, instruments, exemplars, and standards rather than comparing isolated propositions only.
Analyze incommensurability. Identify concepts that change meaning, standards that shift weight, and problems that cease to be problems or become newly visible.
Explain conversion and consolidation. Show why practitioners moved, how the new paradigm opened normal science, and how later textbooks may recast the transition as cumulative.
scope_and_limits: Kuhnian Paradigm Theory is best for historical and sociological analysis of mature sciences undergoing deep conceptual change. It is weaker as a real-time decision rule for accepting or rejecting a theory, and it can understate continuity across revolutions when applied without care. It analyzes communities, exemplars, and standards rather than isolated hypotheses.
source_summary: Authored from model knowledge of Kuhn's account of normal science, anomaly, crisis, incommensurability, and scientific revolutions, without fetched source text.

source_texts.title: Thomas S. Kuhn, The Structure of Scientific Revolutions (1962)
Thomas S. Kuhn, 'Objectivity, Value Judgment, and Theory Choice' (1977)
Thomas S. Kuhn, The Essential Tension (1977)

when_to_use: A scientific field appears to be solving puzzles under shared assumptions rather than debating fundamentals.
Persistent anomalies are accumulating, but practitioners disagree about whether they threaten the field's foundations.
A dispute involves competing standards of evidence, legitimate problems, or meanings of core terms rather than one neutral dataset.
A historical episode looks less like cumulative improvement and more like a reorganization of a community's world-picture.
Participants in a technical debate seem to talk past one another because their exemplars and taxonomies differ.
